获取突发新闻警报和特别报道。重要的新闻和故事会在工作日早上发布。
订阅。
Facebook周六表示，将删除错误的帖子，声称反法西斯活动人士一直在俄勒冈州和其他西部州恶意引发野火。
在此之前，包括俄勒冈州道格拉斯县警长办公室在内的多个组织在社交媒体上就虚假谣言发出警告，另一名警长部门在视频中看到一名副手暗示火灾是由安提法的追随者纵火后，让他休假。
Facebook发言人安迪·斯通在推特上写道：我们正在消除俄勒冈州野火是由某些组织引发的虚假说法。这是基于执法部门的确认，即这些谣言迫使当地消防和警察机构转移用于灭火和保护公众的资源。
官员们表示，周六被行政休假的克拉卡马斯县副警长被拍到谈论纵火的视频时，他的任务是确保居民知道野火的危险。
让我们的新闻与您的收件箱见面。重要的新闻和故事，在工作日的早上递送。
他在一份声明中说，警长办公室的任务是提供平静和安全，特别是在这样史无前例的时刻。我同样期望我们的代表，并向我们社区的所有人道歉。
当局逮捕了41岁的迈克尔·巴克拉(Michael Bakkela)，他涉嫌与俄勒冈州38起活跃野火中的一起有关，这场火灾已造成至少8人死亡。杰克逊县警长官员周五表示，他被指控在俄勒冈州凤凰城的阿尔梅达大火已经燃烧时点燃了灌木丛。
当局还不得不与在马尔特诺马县设置路障的居民作斗争，那里的警长副手敦促居民把执法工作留给他们自己。
周五晚，马尔特诺玛县警长办公室在推特上写道，副警长已经联系了科比特的几个居民团体，他们已经设立了检查站，正在拦截汽车。虽然我们理解他们的意图是为了保护社区的安全，但封锁公共道路或强迫其他公民停车从来都是不合法的。
道格拉斯县警长办公室就社交媒体上流传的虚假谣言发出警告。
该办公室周六在Facebook上表示，谣言像野火一样蔓延，现在我们的9-1-1调度员和专业人员被要求提供信息和询问不真实传言的请求淹没了，该传言称，6名Antifa成员因在俄勒冈州道格拉斯县纵火而被捕。
这不是真的！，它接着说。不幸的是，人们在散布这个谣言，它正在造成问题。
华盛顿的一个消防员工会最近在Facebook上说，这个平台现在绝对是一个错误信息的污水池。
他说，这与我们过去删除可能导致迫在眉睫的伤害的内容的努力是一致的，因为随着大火的肆虐，可能会给人类生命带来风险。
title: Facebook将撤下俄勒冈州安法纵火的虚假报道
title_en: Facebook to take down false reports of antifa arson in Oregon

note:
在大流行期间，天体物理学家除了幻想拥有一个私人黑洞外，还能做些什么呢？
虽然这可能是一厢情愿的想法，但一些天文学家争辩说，我们太阳系的外围可能潜伏着一个黑洞。整个夏天，他们一直在争论如何找到它，如果它真的在那里，以及如何解决它，提出了只有半个世界的计划。
这一猜测始于2016年，当时加州理工学院(California Institute Of Technology)的天文学家迈克尔·布朗(Michael Brown)和康斯坦丁·巴蒂金(Konstantin Batygin)提出，在冥王星以外数十亿英里的地方，几个冰球的奇怪运动可能是一个以前未知和未被怀疑的物体方式的证据，远远地在黑暗中存在。
根据他们的计算，该天体的质量将大约是地球的10倍，并将占据一个蛋形的轨道，使其与太阳的距离接近200亿英里-几倍于太阳到冥王星的距离-并每隔1万到2万年将其带到1000亿英里以外的地方。
“我们不知道的是它在轨道上的位置，这太糟糕了，”布朗博士当时告诉“泰晤士报”。
布朗博士称这个假想的天体为九号行星，其中充满了讽刺意味。不久前，冥王星还被认为是第九颗行星，但布朗博士对柯伊伯带其他外星人的发现在15年前将冥王星降级为矮行星的过程中发挥了重要作用。柯伊伯带是冥王星居住的冰冻的、绕轨道运行的土球的王国。
去年，另两位天文学家--英国达勒姆大学的雅各布·肖尔茨和芝加哥伊利诺伊大学的詹姆斯·昂温--认为九号行星可能实际上是一个黑洞。但不是任何类型的黑洞。
黑洞是阿尔伯特·爱因斯坦(Albert Einstein)方程式所预言的引力恐怖，物体密度如此之高，以至于连光都无法逃脱--这是走向末日的单向通道。天文学家知道这样的实体是存在的。激光干涉仪引力波天文台和处女座天文台听到了黑洞--坍塌的死星的引力壳--在黑暗宇宙中碰撞的声音。一些宇宙学家推测，黑洞可能占宇宙质量的25%，并可能构成著名的、难以捉摸的“暗物质”，它决定了我们在天空中看到的物体的引力结构。
但是你不需要一颗恒星去死才能形成一个黑洞。1971年，斯蒂芬·霍金(Stephen Hawking)借鉴了俄罗斯物理学家雅科夫·鲍里索维奇·泽尔多维奇(Yakov Borisovich Zel‘dovich)和伊戈尔·德米特里耶维奇·诺维科夫(Igor Dmitriyevich Novikov)早些时候在1966年提出的一个观点，提出了大爆炸期间的强烈压力可能会将物质直接坍塌到黑洞的理论。这些原始黑洞可能是任何大小的，也可能在任何地方。一个像地球一样大的黑洞大约有一个乒乓球那么大，非常难以看到。
目前还没有探测到这样的原始黑洞。但也没有排除他们存在的可能性。肖尔茨博士和昂温博士指出，波兰华沙大学的一项名为OGLE的实验，即光学引力透镜实验，在我们银河系中心的方向探测到了六个暗物体的存在。它们的引力场就像透镜一样，短暂地放大了漂浮在它们前面的遥远恒星发出的光。
作者说，这些天体可能是自由漂浮的行星，质量从地球的一半到大约20倍不等。但天文学家提出，它们很可能是漂浮在银河系周围的原始黑洞。如果是这样的话，假设的行星九也很可能是一个位于遥远的太阳轨道上的黑洞。
这将使九号行星成为距离地球许多光年最近的黑洞，距离如此之近，以至于人类可以考虑向那里发送一个机器人探测器，就像新地平线号(New Horizons)经过冥王星和现在被称为阿罗科斯(Arrokoth)的哑铃冰山一样。
但首先我们必须找到九号行星。今年早些时候，普林斯顿高等研究院(Institute For Advanced Study In Princeton)的理论物理学家爱德华·威滕(Edward Witten)也加入了进来；威滕博士是为数不多的获得著名的菲尔兹奖(Fields Medal)的物理学家，他以弦理论方面的研究而闻名，弦理论是有争议的“万物理论”。威滕博士建议借鉴俄罗斯慈善家尤里·米尔纳(Yuri Milner)和霍金博士提出的突破星射(Breakthrough Starshot)的技巧，向最近的半人马座阿尔法星系(Alpha Centauri)发送数千个激光推进的显微探测器。
威滕博士建议向各个方向发射数百个类似的小型探测器，以探索太阳系。通过跟踪来自探测器的传入信号，地球上的科学家将能够分辨出每个探测器在遇到九号行星的引力场或其他任何东西时是否以及何时加速或减速。
这一计划的关键是探测器能够保持精确的每十万分之一秒ping地球的能力。今年5月，马里兰大学(University Of Marland)的天文学家斯科特·劳伦斯(Scott Lawrence)和泽夫·罗戈辛斯基(Zeeve Rogoszinski)建议改用高分辨率射电望远镜监测探测器的轨迹，这将消除对探测器上高精度时钟的需求。
“所有这些都乐观地希望，9号行星确实存在，并被证明是一个黑洞，”威滕博士在一封电子邮件中说，“而且技术发展得足够成熟，有可能制作出合适的”突破之星“的比例版。”
在一封电子邮件中，他的同事尼玛·阿卡尼-哈米德(Nima Arkani-Hamed)也是一位著名的弦理论家，他称这些想法“相当未来主义，但真的很酷！”
今年5月，哈佛大学天文学系主任、突破性星射计划(Breakthrough Starshot Enterprise)科学顾问委员会主席阿维·勒布(Avi Loeb)给这个白日梦泼了一盆冷水。在自己的帖子中，他和韩国科技大学(Korea University Of Science And Technology)的田宏(Thiem Hoang)辩称，星际介质--漂浮在行星和恒星之间的稀薄带电气体--中的摩擦力和电磁力的影响，将淹没来自九号行星的任何引力效应的信号。
但勒布博士很少遇到一个听起来像科幻的理论或项目不引起他的兴趣。他在天文学圈子里广为人知，因为他认为天文学家应该认真对待这样一种可能性，即2017年从星际空间微风掠过太阳系的彗星状物体Oumuamua实际上是一个外星空间探测器。
因此，在7月，勒布博士带着学生阿米尔·西拉杰(Amir Siraj)回来了，并提出了一个寻找行星9黑洞的新想法。他们认为，如果那里存在黑洞，它偶尔会撕裂小彗星，产生明亮的耀斑，很快就会被新的维拉·C·鲁宾天文台(Vera C.Rubin Observatory)发现，该天文台以前被称为大型天气观测望远镜(Large Synotical Survey Telescope)，目前正在智利建造。天文台的任务是从2021年开始拍摄一部宇宙电影，每隔几天制作一次整个南方夜空的全景，并揭示任何变化或移动的东西。
他们指出，这样的耀斑每年应该发生几次。勒布博士在一封电子邮件中写道，“我们的计算显示，耀斑的亮度将足以让维拉·鲁宾天文台(Vera Rubin Observatory)在其L.S.T.调查监测到天空后的一年内，排除或确认九号行星是黑洞。”
此外，他们说，由于鲁宾望远镜检查了如此大片的天空，它可以探测到或排除大小相似的黑洞一直延伸到奥尔特云的可能性，奥尔特云是距离太阳1万亿英里的原始彗星和原始的冰冻残骸的模糊而扩散的集合。
勒布博士在电子邮件中说，在我们自己的太阳系中发现黑洞的前景“就像找到证据表明有人可能住在你家后院的棚子里一样令人震惊”。“如果是，那是谁，它是怎么到那里去的？”
如果这一理论成功了，认为人类可以考虑发射探测器来研究我们当地的黑洞并不是一件疯狂的事情。它会在那里学到什么呢？
对于许多天体物理学家和引力专家来说，当务之急是检验霍金博士46年前的一项预测，即黑洞尽管名为黑洞，但应该以热的形式辐射能量。几乎每个天体物理学家都相信，这一预言--刻在威斯敏斯特大教堂(Westminster Abbey)靠近牛顿和达尔文坟墓的霍金的墓碑上--将会得到证实，但它还没有得到证实。对于像LIGO和处女座一直在记录的那些巨大的黑洞来说，影响将是微不足道的，因此不可能被识别。但较小的黑洞温度更高，而且随着它们的收缩并最终爆炸，它们会变得更热。
根据Witten博士的说法，一个质量约为地球6倍的黑洞的温度约为0.04开尔文(Kelvin)。这比外层空间要冷，外层空间的温度约为3开尔文，而且太冷了，无法从地球上测量。
“从近距离测量它将是一个挑战，”威腾博士指出。“但在本世纪末实现这一目标并不是不可能的。”
他补充说，“我认为人们需要一艘质量相当大的航天器围绕该物体运行，并对其进行详细研究，而不是微型航天器的飞越。”
几年前，在哈佛大学黑洞计划的一次演讲中，勒布博士开玩笑说，在实地考察黑洞的背景下，还有另一种可能性。正如他在一封电子邮件中叙述的那样：“由于黑洞提供了一个罕见的测试弦理论的环境，我建议我的弦理论朋友进入那个黑洞的地平线，在那里测试他们的理论。”尼玛·阿卡尼-哈米德从观众中喊道，我把弦理论家送进黑洞肯定有隐藏的议程。“。
就目前而言，最后一句话属于布朗博士，他是九号行星的发起人，当记者联系到他时，他承认九号行星可能是一个黑洞。“但这没有道理，”他说。“我想，九号行星也有可能是一个6个地球质量的汉堡。”
他补充说，“好消息是，九号行星真的、真的、真的不太可能是黑洞，但一旦我们发现它，我们就可以用这样的探测器来研究它。”
note_en:
What is an astrophysicist to do during a pandemic, except maybe daydream about having a private black hole?
 Although it is probably wishful thinking, some astronomers contend that a black hole may be lurking in the outer reaches of our solar system. All summer, they have been arguing over how to find it, if indeed it is there, and what to do about it, proposing plans that are only halfway out of this world.
 The speculation began back in 2016 when Michael Brown and Konstantin Batygin, astronomers at the California Institute of Technology, proposed that the weird motions of a few ice balls billions of miles beyond Pluto  could be evidence of a previously unknown and unsuspected object way, way out there in the dark.
 According to their calculations, that object would be roughly 10 times as massive as Earth and would occupy an egg-shaped orbit that brought it as near as 20 billion miles from the sun — several times the distance from the sun to Pluto — and took it as far as 100 billion miles away every 10,000 to 20,000 years.
 “What we don’t know is where it is in its orbit, which is too bad,” Dr. Brown told the Times at the time.
 Dr. Brown called this hypothetical object Planet Nine, which is rich in irony. Not long ago, Pluto was considered the ninth planet, but Dr. Brown’s discoveries of other denizens in the Kuiper belt, the realm of frozen, orbiting dirt balls that Pluto inhabits, played a major role in  demoting Pluto to a dwarf planet 15 years ago.
  Last year, another pair of astronomers — Jakub Scholtz of Durham University in Britain and James Unwin of the University of Illinois at Chicago — suggested that Planet Nine might actually be a black hole. But not just any kind of black hole.
 Black holes are the gravitational terrors predicted by Albert Einstein’s equations, objects so dense that not even light can escape from them — one-way passages to doom. Astronomers know that such entities exist. The Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory and the Virgo observatory have heard black holes — the gravitational shells of collapsed dead stars — banging together out in the dark cosmos. Some cosmologists have speculated that black holes could account for 25 percent of the mass of the universe and could constitute the famous and elusive “dark matter” that determines the gravitational structure of what we see in the sky.
 But you don’t need a star to die to make a black hole. In 1971, Stephen Hawking, drawing on an idea earlier suggested in 1966 by the Russian physicists  Yakov Borisovich Zel’dovich and  Igor Dmitriyevich Novikov, theorized that intense pressures during the Big Bang could have collapsed matter directly into black holes. Those primordial black holes could be of any size and could be anywhere. A black hole as massive as Earth would be about the size of a Ping-Pong ball and would be exceptionally hard to see.
 No such primordial black holes have been detected yet. But neither has their existence been ruled out. Dr. Scholtz and Dr. Unwin pointed out that an experiment called OGLE, for Optical Gravitational Lensing Experiment, based at the University of Warsaw in Poland, had detected the presence of a half-dozen dark objects in the direction of the center of our Milky Way galaxy. Their gravitational fields had acted as lenses, briefly amplifying the light from distant stars that they drifted in front of.
 Those objects could be free-floating planets, the authors said, with masses ranging from half to about 20 times that of Earth. But they could as easily be primordial black holes floating around the galaxy, the astronomers proposed. If that were the case, the putative Planet Nine could well be a black hole, too, in a distant orbit around the sun.
  That would make Planet Nine the nearest black hole to Earth by many light-years, so close that humans could contemplate sending a robot probe there, much as New Horizons has passed Pluto and the dumbbell iceberg now known as Arrokoth four billion miles from here.
 But first we must find Planet Nine. Earlier this year, Edward Witten, a theoretical physicist at the Institute for Advanced Study in Princeton, chimed in; Dr. Witten is the rare physicist who has won the prestigious Fields Medal in mathematics and is known, among other things, for his work on string theory, the controversial “theory of everything.” Dr. Witten suggested borrowing a trick from  Breakthrough Starshot, the proposal by Russian philanthropist Yuri Milner and Dr. Hawking to send thousands of laser-propelled microscopic probes to the nearest star system, Alpha Centauri.
 Dr. Witten  suggested sending hundreds of similarly small probes outward in all directions to explore the solar system. By keeping track of incoming signals from the probes, scientists on Earth would be able to tell if and when each one sped up or slowed down as it encountered the gravitational field of Planet Nine or anything else out there.
 Key to this plan would be the ability of the probes to keep pinging Earth precisely every hundred-thousandth of a second. In May, astronomers Scott Lawrence and Zeeve Rogoszinski of the University of Maryland suggested instead monitoring the trajectories of the probes with high-resolution radio telescopes, which would obviate the need for high-precision clocks on the probes.
 “All this is optimistically hoping that Planet 9 does exist and turns out to be a black hole,” Dr. Witten said in an email, “and that technology develops enough that a suitable scaled version of Breakthrough Starshot is possible.”
 In an email, his colleague Nima Arkani-Hamed, also a prominent string theorist, called these ideas “pretty futuristic, but really cool!”
  In May, Avi Loeb, chair of the astronomy department at Harvard and leader of a scientific advisory board for the Breakthrough Starshot enterprise, poured cold water on that daydream. In their  own posting, he and Thiem Hoang of the Korea University of Science and Technology argued that the effects of friction and electromagnetic forces in the interstellar medium — the dilute electrified gas that wafts among the planets and stars — would swamp the signal from any gravitational effects from Planet Nine.
 But Dr. Loeb has rarely met a sci-fi-sounding theory or project that didn’t intrigue him. He is well known in astronomical circles for arguing that astronomers should take seriously the possibility that Oumuamua, the cometlike object that breezed through the solar system from interstellar space in 2017, was actually an alien space probe.
 So in July Dr. Loeb was back, with a student, Amir Siraj, and  a new idea for finding the Planet Nine black hole. If a black hole were out there, they argued, it would occasionally rip apart small comets, causing bright flares that could soon be spotted by the new Vera C. Rubin Observatory, previously known as the Large Synoptic Survey Telescope, now under construction in Chile. The observatory’s mission, starting in 2021, is to make a movie of the universe, producing a panorama of the entire southern night sky every few days and revealing anything that has changed or moved.
 Such flares should occur a few times a year, they noted. “Our calculations show that the flares will be bright enough for the Vera Rubin Observatory to rule out or confirm Planet Nine as a black hole within one year of monitoring the sky with its L.S.S.T. survey,” Dr. Loeb wrote in an email.
 Moreover, because the Rubin telescope examines such a large swath of sky, it could detect or rule out black holes of similar size all the way out to the Oort cloud, a vague and diffuse assemblage of protocomets and primordial, frozen riffraff a trillion miles from the sun, they said.
 The prospect of finding a black hole in our own solar system “is as startling as finding evidence that someone might be living in the shed in your backyard,” Dr. Loeb said in the email. “If so, who is it, and how did it get there?”
  If the theory pans out, it’s not crazy to think that humans could contemplate sending a probe to study our local black hole. What would it learn there?
 A top priority for many astrophysicists and gravity experts would be to test a prediction made by Dr. Hawking 46 years ago, that black holes, despite their name, should radiate energy in the form of heat. Almost every astrophysicist believes that the prediction — which is inscribed on Dr. Hawking’s tombstone in Westminster Abbey, near the graves of Newton and Darwin — will be confirmed, but it has yet to be. The effect would be beyond minuscule for the giant black holes like those that LIGO and Virgo have been recording, and thus impossible to discern. But smaller black holes are hotter, and they grow hotter still as they shrink and finally explode.
 A black hole of about six times Earth’s mass would have a temperature of about 0.04 of a degree Kelvin, according to Dr. Witten. That is colder than outer space, which is about 3 degrees Kelvin, and much too cool to measure from Earth.
 “It would be a challenge to measure it from up close,” Dr. Witten noted. “But it is not out of the question that it could be done by century’s end.”
 He added, “I believe one would need a spacecraft of substantial mass orbiting the object and studying it in detail, not a flyby by a miniature spacecraft.”
 In a talk at Harvard’s Black Hole Initiative a couple of years ago, Dr. Loeb jested about another possibility in the context of a field trip to a black hole. As he recounted in an email: “Since black holes offer a rare environment where string theory can be tested, I recommended to my string-theory friends to enter the horizon of that black hole and test their theory there. Nima Arkani-Hamed shouted from the audience that I must have a hidden agenda for sending string theorists into a black hole.”
 For now, the last word belongs Dr. Brown, the promoter of Planet Nine, who, when reached, conceded it was possible that Planet Nine was a black hole. “But it doesn’t make sense,” he said. “It is also possible that Planet Nine is a six-Earth-mass hamburger, I guess.”
 He added, “The good news is that Planet Nine is really, really, really unlikely to be a black hole but that we can use probes like this to study it once we find it.”
title: 行星九是黑洞吗？
title_en: Is Planet Nine a Black Hole?

note:
全球大型IT服务提供商Virtusa签署了一项最终的合并协议，根据协议，它将被私募股权公司霸菱私募股权亚洲公司(Bling Private Equity Asia)收购，交易价值约20亿美元，全现金交易。
根据周四公布的交易条款，总部位于香港的私募股权公司霸菱亚洲私募股权投资公司(Bling Private Equity Asia)的附属基金将以每股51.25美元的价格收购总部位于马萨诸塞州南伯勒的Virtusa，这比Virtusa周三的收盘价溢价约27%。
霸菱亚洲私募股权投资公司(BPEA)成立于1997年，管理着约200亿美元的资产。该公司在中国和亚洲其他地区设有办事处，自成立以来已投资了100多家公司。
在BPEA目前投资的41家公司中，有一些专注于IT的公司，包括总部设在新泽西州普林斯顿的医疗保健技术和商业服务提供商CitiusTech；总部位于印度诺伊达的全球IT解决方案和服务公司Coforge(前身为NIIT Technologies)；以及总部位于印度孟买的全球IT咨询和数字解决方案提供商Hexware Technologies。
Virtusa与广泛的供应商合作伙伴合作，提供数字战略、数字工程以及IT服务和解决方案。主要合作伙伴包括Amazon Web Services Premier、Google Cloud Premier和Microsoft Azure Gold合作伙伴关系。该公司在其网站上表示，其员工中有100多名接受过云培训和认证的工程师和SaaS顾问。
Virtusa的核心服务包括咨询和系统设计、应用工程、分析和数据、数字流程自动化、企业应用集成、云服务和托管服务。
然而，Virtusa董事长兼首席执行官克里斯·卡内克拉特内(Kris Canekeratne)在一份事先准备好的声明中表示，在数字采用加速之际，此次收购是将其业务推向新高度的独特机会。
BPEA在重视创新和有才华的全球团队以及支持和授权其投资的企业方面有着堪称典范的记录。有了BPEA的强大合作伙伴，我们将在未来几年巩固我们在数字转型前沿的地位，“Canekeratne在声明中说。
note_en:
Virtusa, a large global IT services provider, has signed a definitive merger agreement under which it will be acquired by a  private equity firm, Baring Private Equity Asia, in an all-cash deal worth about $2.0 billion.
 Under the terms of the deal, which was unveiled Thursday, funds affiliated with Baring Private Equity Asia, a Hong Kong-based private equity company, will acquire Southborough, Mass.-based Virtusa for $51.25 per share, which represents a premium of about 27 percent over Wednesday’s closing share price for Virtusa.
 Baring Private Equity Asia, or BPEA, was founded in 1997 and has about $20 billion of assets under management. The company has offices throughout China and the rest of Asia, and has invested in over 100 companies since it was founded.
  Among BPEA’s 41 current portfolio companies are a few IT-focused companies including CitiusTech, a Princeton, N.J.-based provider of health-care technology and business services; Coforge, a Noida, India-based global IT solutions and services company formerly known as NIIT Technologies; and Hexaware Technologies, a Mumbai, India-based provider of global IT consulting and digital solutions.
 Virtusa works with a wide range of vendor partners to provide digital strategy, digital engineering, and IT services and solutions. Key relationships include Amazon Web Services Premier, Google Cloud Premier and Microsoft Azure Gold partnerships. Among its employees are over 100 cloud- trained and -certified engineers and SaaS consultants, the company said on its website.
 Virtusa’s core services include consulting and systems design, application engineering, analytics and data, digital process automation, enterprise application integration, cloud services and managed services.
   However, Kris Canekeratne, Virtusa chairman and CEO, said in a prepared statement that the acquisition is a unique opportunity to take its business to new heights at a time of accelerating digital adoption.
 “BPEA has an exemplary track record of valuing innovative and talented global teams and supporting and empowering the businesses in which it invests. With a strong partner in BPEA, we will solidify our position at the forefront of  digital transformation for years to come,” Canekeratne said in the statement.
title: IT服务公司Virtusa将被霸菱私募股权亚洲公司以全现金交易收购，交易价值约20亿美元
title_en: IT service company Virtusa to be acquired by Baring Private Equity Asia in an all-cash deal worth about $2B

note:
我们认为，互联网需要一种全球原生货币。就像每个人都应该能够参与经济并能够获得相同的工具和服务一样，每个人都应该能够参与加密货币并能够获得其根本创新。
在Square，我们致力于将我们的加密货币基础技术提供给每个人使用。加密货币技术及其采用仍处于初级阶段。我们认为，加密货币的成功取决于社区走到一起，在现有技术的基础上建设和发展创新，当各方在专利和诉讼中捆绑基础技术时，这是不可能的。虽然专利有时可能用于防御目的，但对专利的攻击性和误导性使用威胁到加密货币等新兴技术的增长和采用。有人担心，少数人对基础加密货币技术的“专利锁定”将扼杀创新，阻止大规模采用。
这就是为什么我们兴奋地宣布启动加密货币开放专利联盟(COPA)，邀请加密社区中的每个人加入一个开放专利联盟，以解决这些担忧。COPA使用双重方法解决专利锁定问题。首先，COPA成员承诺，除非出于防御原因，否则永远不会对任何人使用他们的密码专利，这实际上是让所有人都可以免费使用这些专利。其次，COPA创建了一个共享专利库，成员将他们所有的加密专利汇集在一起，形成一个专利的集体盾牌，允许成员使用彼此的专利来威慑和防御专利侵略者。这有助于使每个人的专利大众化，即使是小公司也有保护自己的工具和杠杆。
随着更多的会员加入并声明他们不会冒犯他人主张自己的专利，专利诉讼的总体发生率和威胁就会下降。拥有专利往往是抵御专利侵略者的最好威慑，但并不是每家公司都拥有或能够获得专利。共享专利库为每个成员提供了这个盾牌，随着更多的成员加入，这个盾牌会变得越来越大和坚固-无论他们是大是小，无论他们自己是否拥有专利-最终使整个社区受益。
我们希望COPA将改变人们在密码世界中看待和使用专利的方式，它将帮助专利为加密货币的进一步创新和进步做出贡献，而不是成为运作的障碍。我们设想该组织将随着时间的推移而不断发展，并期待着来自社区的讨论和反馈来实现这一使命。
note_en:
We believe there needs to be a global native currency for the internet. Just as everyone should be able to participate in the economy and have access to the same tools and services, so too should everyone be able to participate in cryptocurrencies and have access to its underlying innovation.
  At Square, we are committed to making our foundational technology on cryptocurrency available for everyone to use. Cryptocurrency technology and its adoption is still at a nascent stage. We believe that cryptocurrency’s success depends on the community coming together to build and develop upon existing technologies to innovate, which is not possible when parties tie up foundational technology in patents and litigation. While patents may at times be useful for defensive purposes, offensive and misguided use of patents threatens the growth and adoption of emerging technologies such as cryptocurrencies. There is concern that “patent lockup” of foundational cryptocurrency technologies by a select few will stifle innovation and deter mass-adoption.
  That’s why we’re excited to announce the launch of the Cryptocurrency Open Patent Alliance (COPA), an invitation to everyone in the crypto community to join an open patent alliance to address these concerns. COPA tackles the patent lockup concern using a dual approach. First, COPA members pledge never to use their crypto patents against anyone, except for defensive reasons, effectively making these patents freely available for all to use. Second, COPA creates a shared patent library where members pool all of their crypto patents together to form a collective shield of patents, allowing members to use each others’ patents to deter and defend against patent aggressors. This helps democratize patents for everyone, empowering even small companies with tools and leverage to defend themselves.
  As more members join and declare that they won’t offensively assert their patents against others, the overall incidence and threat of patent litigation comes down. Owning patents are often the best deterrents to ward off patent aggressors but not every company possesses or has access to patents. The shared patent library provides this shield, which gets larger and stronger as more members join, to every member – regardless of whether they are small or large, whether they own patents themselves or not – ultimately benefiting the entire community.
  We hope that COPA will transform the way patents are viewed and used in the crypto world, and that it will help patents contribute to further innovation and advancement of cryptocurrencies rather than operate as obstacles. We envision the organization evolving over time, and look forward to discussions and feedback from the community to realize this mission.
title: 密码开放专利联盟

note:
今年早些时候，旧金山湾区一家小剧院的组织者告诉艾丽西亚·达特纳(Alicia Dattner)，他们无法支付她即将上演的喜剧表演的费用。原因是：加州的一项新法律将零工经济工人(如优步司机)重新归类为员工，这意味着雇用她的成本太高。
达特纳女士很困惑。为什么一项针对优步和Lyft的法律会影响喜剧演员？
note_en:
Early this year, the organizers of a small Bay Area theater told Alicia Dattner they wouldn’t be able to pay her for an upcoming comedy act. The reason: A new California law that reclassified gig-economy workers, such as Uber drivers, as employees meant it would be too expensive to hire her.
  Ms. Dattner was confused. Why was a law aimed at Uber and Lyft affecting comedians?
title: 加州法律将给予优步司机新的保护。相反，喜剧演员丢掉了工作
title_en: CA Law Was to Give Uber Drivers New Protections. Instead, Comedians Lost Work

note:
Facebook在2015年末首次收到警告，称剑桥分析公司滥用从数百万美国人非法获得的数据，试图影响2016年美国大选。
直到2018年3月，“卫报”的报道将这起泄密事件变成了一场全球丑闻后，谷歌才停止了对该公司用户数据的访问。
两年多过去了--距离2020年大选投票截止日期只有两个月了--Facebook决定更多地了解它对民主的影响，上周宣布将与17名研究人员合作，研究Facebook和Instagram对选民态度和行为的影响。
但项目外的研究人员感到矛盾。虽然他们称赞Facebook承诺确保比以前更多的透明度和独立性，但他们也质疑公司为什么等了这么久，以及这项研究到底会带来多大的影响。
非营利研究组织Avaaz的活动总监法迪·古兰(Fadi Quran)告诉“商业内幕”(Business Insider)，这是不是有点太晚了？
他说，Facebook很早以前就知道有选举干预，恶意分子正在利用这个平台影响选民。为什么这件事现在才发生在这么晚的阶段？
Facebook表示，它预计最早要到2021年年中才会公布任何发现。该公司没有回复对这篇报道的置评请求。
由于公司让研究团队决定问哪些问题并得出他们自己的结论--这是一件好事--我们还不太清楚他们希望了解什么。Facebook在最初的声明中表示，它很好奇：社交媒体是否会让我们作为一个社会变得更加两极分化，或者它是否在很大程度上反映了已经存在的分歧；它是否帮助人们更好地了解政治，还是减少了；或者它是否影响了人们对政府和民主的态度，包括他们是否投票以及如何投票。
据报道，Facebook的高管们已经知道第一个问题的答案--该公司的算法确实帮助人们两极分化和激进化--而且他们故意停止解决这个问题的努力，甚至更深入地研究这个问题。
但即使撇开这一点不谈，研究人员表示，他们已经发现了这项研究中的一些潜在缺陷。
纽约大学(New York University)研究政治广告和错误信息的研究员劳拉·埃德尔森(Laura Edelson)在接受“商业内幕”(Business Insider)采访时表示，这项工作的很多焦点很大程度上是关于玩家如何诚实地使用这些系统。
她补充说，我担心的是，他们几乎完全没有关注事情出错的方式，而这正是我希望这种情况能进一步发展的地方。
“古兰经”呼应了这一评价，他说：如果不更深入地审视这些恶毒的行为者，单从设计上看，他们就会错过一件大事，那就是这些行为者创造的内容的规模，以及这些内容对公众舆论的影响。
根据Avaaz的数据，一长串研究和媒体报道记录了Facebook在有效地将政治错误信息排除在其平台上的努力，更不用说误导性的健康声明了。尽管Facebook采取了更激进的做法，但其浏览量仍是推送准确信息的网站帖子的四倍。
但政治信息要微妙得多，也在不断演变，据报道，即使在看似明确的情况下，Facebook有时也会错误地执行自己的政策，或者为了避免可能的政治反弹而后退。
古兰(Quran)和埃德森(Edelson)都担心，Facebook的选举研究可能无法全面反映该平台各方面的影响，比如其算法、数十亿个虚假账户或私人团体。
埃德森说：你找到了你要找的东西。Facebook上选举的大问题不在于诚实的参与者如何在系统内工作。
“古兰经”还表示，虽然现在说这肯定会发生还为时过早，但由于Facebook直接在他们的应用程序中邀请用户加入这项研究，有时是为了换取报酬，它可能会无意中筛选出一开始就不信任该公司的人。
他说：我们已经在不同的群体上看到了分享虚假信息的帖子，告诉人们：不要参与这项研究，这是Facebook的阴谋，目的是监视用户或让共和党人在大选前离开竞选平台。这可能导致的潜在结果是，受虚假信息影响最大的人甚至不是研究的一部分。
埃德森说，在最好的情况下，研究人员可以了解到关于我们对选举的现有理解如何映射到数字世界的有价值的信息。古兰经表示，这项研究甚至可以作为类似于环境影响研究的信息生态系统影响评估，帮助Facebook了解它可能做出的改变可能会如何影响民主进程。
但两人都怀疑Facebook是否会基于这项研究或更广泛的2020年选举做出重大改变。“古兰经”还警告说，尽管Facebook努力使这项研究独立，但人们不应该认为这项研究是确凿的，也不应该让它成为认可的印章。
从了解剑桥分析开始，Facebook花了近四年的时间才发现数以万计的应用程序也在滥用数据。尽管它刚刚公布了第一次独立民权审计的结果，但该公司几乎没有承诺实施任何审计师的建议。

note_en:
Facebook was  first warned in late 2015 that Cambridge Analytica was misusing data illicitly harvested from millions of Americans in an attempt to sway the 2016 US elections.
 It didn't pull the plug on the firm's access to user data until March 2018 after  reporting from The Guardian turned the breach into a global scandal.
 More than two years later — and barely two months before the deadline for votes to cast their ballots in the 2020 elections — Facebook has decided it wants to know more about how it impacts democracy, announcing last week that it would partner with 17 researchers to  study the impact of Facebook and Instagram on voters' attitudes and actions.
 But researchers outside of the project are conflicted. While they praised Facebook for promising to ensure more transparency and independence than it has before, they also questioned why the company waited so long and just how much this study will really bring to light.
   "Isn't this a little bit too late?" Fadi Quran, a campaign director with nonprofit research group Avaaz, told Business Insider.
 "Facebook has known now for a long time that there's election interference, that malicious actors are using the platform to influence voters," he said. "Why is this only happening now at such a late stage?"
 Facebook said it doesn't "expect to publish any findings until mid-2021 at the earliest." The company did not reply to a request for comment on this story.
 Since the company is leaving it to the research team to decide which questions to ask and draw their own conclusions — a good thing — we don't yet know much about what they hope to learn. In its initial announcement, Facebook said it's curious about: "whether social media makes us more polarized as a society, or if it largely reflects the divisions that already exist; if it helps people to become better informed about politics, or less; or if it affects people's attitudes towards government and democracy, including whether and how they vote."
   Facebook executives have reportedly known the answer to that first question — that the company's algorithms do help polarize and radicalize people — and that they  knowingly shut down efforts to fix the issue or even research it more.
 But even setting that aside, researchers say they've already identified some potential shortcomings in the study.
 "A lot of the focus of this work is very much about how honest players are using these systems," Laura Edelson, a researcher who studies political ads and misinformation at New York University, told Business Insider.
 "Where I'm concerned is that they're almost exclusively not looking at the ways that things are going wrong, and that's where I wish this was going further," she added.
   Quran echoed that assessment, saying: "One big thing that they're going to miss by not looking more deeply at these malicious actors, and just by the design, is the scale of content that's been created by these actors and that's influencing public opinion."
 A long list of research and media reports have documented Facebook's struggles to effectively keep political misinformation off its platform — let alone misleading health claims, which despite Facebook's more aggressive approach, still racked up four times as many views as posts from sites pushing accurate information, according to  Avaaz.
 But political information is much more nuanced and constantly evolving, and even in what seem to be clear-cut cases, Facebook has, according to reports, at times  incorrectly enforced its own policies or bent over backward to avoid  possible political backlash.
 Quran and Edelson both worried that Facebook's election study may not capture the full impact of aspects of the platform like its algorithms,  billions of fake accounts, or  private groups.
   "You find what you go and you look for," Edelson said. "The great problem of elections on Facebook is not how the honest actors are working within the system."
 Quran also said, though it's too early say this will happen for sure, that because it's Facebook asking users directly within their apps to join the study, sometimes  in exchange for payment, it risks inadvertently screening out people who are distrustful of the company to begin with.
 "We're already seeing posts on different groups that share disinformation telling people: 'Don't participate in the study, this is a Facebook conspiracy'" to spy on users or keep Republicans off the platform ahead of the election, he said. "What this could lead to, potentially, is that the people most impacted by disinformation are not even part of the study."
 In a best-case scenario, Edelson said the researchers could learn valuable information about how our existing understanding of elections maps onto the digital world. Quran said the study could even serve as an "information ecosystem impact assessment," similar to environmental impact studies, that would help Facebook understand how changes it could make might impact the democratic process.
   But both were skeptical that Facebook would make major changes based on this study or the 2020 elections more broadly. And Quran warned that, despite Facebook's efforts to make the study independent, people shouldn't take the study as definitive or allow it to become a "stamp of approval."
 It took Facebook nearly four years from when it learned about Cambridge Analytica to identify the  tens of thousands of apps that were also misusing data. And though it just published the results of its  first independent civil rights audit, the company has made few commitments to implement any of the auditors' recommendations.

title: Facebook选举研究来得太晚，重点错误：研究人员
title_en: Facebook election study comes too late, has wrong focus: researchers

note:
一名21岁的大学生，从高中开始就没有和她的母亲说过话。
不能与儿媳相处，因此不能与儿子接触的女人。
家庭疏远-一个曾经令人痛心和羞耻的话题，以至于人们在讨论它时犹豫不决-现在吸引了更多的关注，因为一些人讲述了他们的故事，研究人员深入研究了它的原因和后果。
康奈尔大学(Cornell University)的家庭社会学家卡尔·皮勒默(Karl Pillemer)刚刚出版了“断层线：破碎的家庭和如何修补它们”，这本书提供了这一领域中罕见的东西-实际数据。
他问一项有代表性的全国性调查的参与者，“有没有你没有联系过的亲戚？”在回答了一份在线问卷的1340人中，相当多的27%的人报告说与家人疏远了。一半的人已经疏远了四年或更长时间。
在五年的时间里，皮莱默博士和他的同事对与父母、成年子女、兄弟姐妹或其他亲属关系疏远的人进行了数百次采访。他们还采访了许多和解的人，皮勒默博士在他的书中传达了他们的建议。
(旧金山湾区的心理学家约书亚·科尔曼(Joshua Coleman)写的另一本关于这个话题的书“疏远规则”(Rules Of Leangement)将于3月出版。)。
我与皮勒默博士通了电话，谈到了他的发现。我们的对话经过编辑和浓缩。
保拉·斯潘：我们似乎听到了更多关于疏远的说法，而在很长一段时间里，这似乎都是人们不愿谈论的事情。
卡尔·皮莱默：关于它的科学文献如此之少，这让我很吃惊。
但高调的名人们把这一点带到了风口浪尖。哈里王子和梅根·马克尔。安吉丽娜·朱莉与父亲乔恩·沃伊特的关系疏远是出了名的。塔拉·韦斯特弗的书“受过教育”
当家人住得更近，有更多的日常互动时，疏远可能就不那么常见了，这是一种不惜一切代价保持联系的社会规范。当我采访年长的人时，他们经常说无论如何都要和家人在一起。
对于婴儿潮一代和更年轻的人来说，更多的是一种感觉，如果关系不顺利，他们可以继续前进。
这种与家人隔绝或被隔绝的现象在美国非常普遍。
此外，当考虑到性别、种族或教育水平时，你没有发现差异-这可能发生在任何人身上。你能解释一下你所谓的途径，最常见的疏远原因或解释吗？
其一是艰难的儿童史：虐待父母，严厉的父母教育，父母偏袒的记忆--人们并不总是能忘记这些。他们带着他们进入成年期。
第二，离婚，无论它出现在生命周期的什么时候。研究显示，孩子更有可能与父亲失去联系，但这种中断可能会削弱与父母双方的联系。
还有，有问题的姻亲。在数量惊人的案例中，原籍家庭中的某些人认为你嫁错了人，你自己的家庭和你的伴侣的要求之间的典型冲突无法解决。
然后就有钱了。人们对遗产的分配方式有很多不满。你可以把钱分给你的孩子，但不能把传家宝或避暑别墅之类的有形财产分给他们。但出错的商业交易也可能起到作用，或者贷款无法偿还。
和未实现的期望。一个典型的例子涉及照顾年迈的父母：兄弟姐妹A被留给所有的照顾，而兄弟姐妹B什么也不做，所以兄弟姐妹A说，“我不干了。”
最后，生活方式和价值观的差异，特别是在亲子关系上。一个孩子出柜是男同性恋或女同性恋。宗教皈依。不同的政治。
你指出，当人们回顾哪里出了问题时，他们对过去有不同的看法。他们甚至不能就实际发生了什么或谁说了什么达成一致。
正确的。相信一个兄弟姐妹，一个父母，一个成年的孩子会接受你对过去事件的看法是不现实的。但这几乎是一个不可磨灭的愿望。人们经常处于长期的隔阂中，因为另一个人被认为看不到过去的现实。
我们从心理学上知道，我们热爱我们自己的叙述，而且我们不会放弃它们。你不会让感觉自己在情感上受到虐待的姐姐和认为自己只是在进行正常取笑的哥哥的观点一致。
人们认为疏远是孤立和可耻的。他们经常会感到内疚。而且还有污名相随。其他人认为你的家庭有问题。
分析调查数据，疏远与焦虑、抑郁或孤立之间存在相关性。
你的“调解人”--在你的面试样本中大约有100人--与其他人没有明显的不同，不是吗？
他们在造成隔阂的原因、令人心烦意乱的程度以及持续了多长时间方面非常相似。如果我给你看隔阂是如何发生的，以及它有多困难，你就无法区分那些最终和解的人和那些没有和解的人。
情况变了，或者人变了。如果这个问题是一个有问题的姻亲，而且离婚了，那么障碍就不再存在了。
或者人们开始感受到有限时间范围的压力。观察自己或他人的健康问题让他们认为自己不能再拖延了。
只是时间的流逝。它让一些愤怒的情绪消散了。我的一位受访者说，“孩子，开始这件事的争论现在看起来太微不足道了。”
你的调解人提供了一些有用的策略，其中之一就是放弃过去。这并不意味着你原谅和忘记，而是你接受你和另一个人永远不会对所发生的事情有相同的看法。
和解的人将这种经历描述为放弃了让另一个人看到他们所看到的过去的企图。
和解通常是不完美的，即使它是好的。因此，确定你在这段关系中能接受的最低限度是一项非常有用的练习。
在大多数情况下，它确实涉及到以更低的价格达成和解。回到这段关系中仍然是值得的。
调解人制定了非常明确的条款，明确了关系可以存在的具体条件。“如果你在我家，就不能说我丈夫的坏话。这是规则。“。
我知道一些读者会回应说，他们觉得完全有理由切断联系。任何敦促他们和解的人，或者只是告诉他们如何和解的人，都不接受他们的观点，即他们做了正确的事情。
我并不是建议个人和解。但对于绝大多数这样做的人来说，这被证明是一次积极的、有时甚至改变人生的经历。他们发现这是一项重大的人生成就。
与一位近亲完全疏远，并认为这是一件积极的事情，他们很高兴发生了的人，肯定是少数。
我要对那些认为这是他们做过的最好的事情，并因此感到解放的人们说：给你更多的权力。但对于大多数处于疏远状态的人来说，这不是他们的经历。他们觉得自己的生活中缺少了一些东西。
note_en:
A 21-year-old college student who hasn’t spoken to her mother since high school.
 A woman who cannot get along with her daughter-in-law, and who therefore has no contact with her son.
  Family estrangement — a topic once so distressing and shameful that people hesitated to discuss it — is drawing more attention as some tell their stories and researchers delve into its causes and consequences.
 Karl Pillemer, a family sociologist at Cornell University, has just published  “Fault Lines: Fractured Families and How to Mend Them,” a book that provides something rare in this realm — actual data.
 He asked participants in a representative national survey, “Is there a relative with whom you have no contact?” Among the 1,340 people who answered an online questionnaire, a substantial 27 percent reported being estranged from a family member. And half had been estranged for four years or more.
 During a five-year period, Dr. Pillemer and his colleagues conducted hundreds of interviews with people estranged from their parents, adult children, siblings or other relatives. They also interviewed many who had reconciled, and Dr. Pillemer has passed along their advice in his book.
 (Another book on the topic,  “Rules of Estrangement” by Joshua Coleman, a psychologist in the Bay Area, is coming in March.)
 I spoke by phone with Dr. Pillemer about his findings. Our conversation has been edited and condensed.
  Paula Span:  We appear to be hearing more about estrangement, when for so long it seemed to be something people just didn’t talk about.
 Karl Pillemer: It was astonishing to me to find so little scientific literature on it.
 But high-profile celebrities have brought it to the forefront. Prince Harry and Meghan Markle. Angelina Jolie, famously estranged from her father, Jon Voight. Tara Westover’s book, “Educated.”
 Estrangement may have been less common when families lived closer to one another and there was more routine interaction, a social norm that you maintain contact at all costs. When I interview older people, they often describe hanging in with their families no matter what.
 With the baby boomers and younger, there’s more of a sense that if the relationship’s not working out, they can move on.
 This phenomenon of cutting off or being cut off from a family member is strikingly common in America.
 Plus, you didn’t find differences when considering gender or race or education level — this can happen to anyone. Can you explain what you call pathways, the most common reasons or explanations for estrangement?
 One is difficult childhood histories: Abusive parenting, harsh parenting, memories of parental favoritism — people don’t always get over those. They carry them into adulthood.
 Second, divorce, no matter when it appears in the life cycle. Children are more likely to lose contact with fathers, the research shows, but the disruption can weaken the ties to both parents.
 Also, the problematic in-law. In a striking number of cases, someone in the family of origin thinks you’ve married the wrong person, and the classic conflict between the demands of your own family and your partner can’t be resolved.
  Then there’s money. There’s lots of resentment around how inheritances are distributed. You can divide your money among your kids, but you can’t divide tangible property like heirlooms or a summer house. But also business deals gone wrong can contribute, or loans unrepaid.
 And unmet expectations. An archetypal example involves caregiving for aging parents: Sibling A is left with all the care and Sibling B doesn’t do anything, so Sibling A says, “I’m done.”
 Finally, lifestyle and value discrepancies, especially in parent and child relationships. A kid coming out as gay or lesbian. A religious conversion. Different politics.
 You point out that when people look back at what went wrong, they have divergent views of the past. They can’t even agree on what actually occurred or who said what.
 Right. It’s not a realistic expectation to believe that a sibling, a parent, an adult child is going to come over to your view of these past events. But it’s an almost indelible wish. People are often in long-term estrangements because the other person supposedly can’t see the reality of the past.
 We know from psychology that we love our own narratives and we don’t give them up. You’re not going to align the perspectives of the sister who felt she was emotionally abused and the brother who thought he was just doing normal teasing.
 People experience estrangement as isolating and shameful. They often experience guilt. And there’s stigma attached. Other people think there’s something wrong with your family.
 Analyzing the survey data, there were correlations between being estranged and feeling anxious or depressed or isolated.
 Your “reconcilers” — about 100 of them in your interview sample — weren’t obviously different from the others, were they?
 They were remarkably similar in what caused the estrangement, how upsetting it was and how long it had gone on. If I showed you accounts of how the estrangement occurred and how difficult it was, you wouldn’t be able to distinguish between those who eventually reconciled and those that haven’t.
  The situation had changed or the person had changed. If the issue was a problematic in-law and there was a divorce, the barrier wasn’t there anymore.
 Or people began to feel the pressure of a limited time horizon. Observing their own or others’ health problems made them think they could no longer put it off.
 And just the passage of time. It let some of the angry feelings dissipate. One of my interviewees said, “Boy, the argument that started it seems so trivial now.”
 Your reconcilers offered some helpful strategies, one of which was letting go of the past. They don’t mean that you forgive and forget, but that you accept that you and the other person won’t ever have the same view of what happened.
 People who reconcile describe the experience as letting go of the attempt to have the other person see the past as they saw it.
  Reconciliation is usually imperfect, even if it’s good. So determining the least you can accept in the relationship was a very useful exercise.
 It did involve settling for less, in most cases. It was still worth it to be back in the relationship.
  The reconcilers developed very clear terms, specific conditions under which the relationship could exist. “If you’re in my house, you can’t say anything negative about my husband. That’s the rule.”
 I know some readers will respond that they feel fully justified in cutting off contact. And that anyone urging them to reconcile — or simply telling them how to reconcile — doesn’t accept their view that they did the right thing.
 I’m not recommending that individuals reconcile. But for the vast majority who do, it turned out to be a positive, sometimes even life-changing experience. They found it to be a major life accomplishment.
 The number of people who were completely estranged from a close relative and identified that as a positive event, one they were glad had occurred, were certainly a minority.
 I would say to the people who feel that it was the best thing they ever did and they feel liberated as a result: More power to you. But for most people in estrangement, that’s not their experience. They feel there’s something missing from their lives.
title: 疏远的原因，以及家庭是如何愈合的
title_en: The Causes of Estrangement, and How Families Heal
